summary: As major Python packages like NumPy, Pandas, and Scikit-Learn prepare to drop Python 2 support, many large institutions will face painful transitions. The post explores an unconventional solution: using Cython to compile Python 3 codebases into C extensions usable by Python 2. While technically feasible, this approach has limitations including incompatibility with Python 3-only libraries, semantic differences, and debugging difficulties. Rocklin ultimately suggests this could be a viable transitional tool, particularly as a commercial offering from companies like Anaconda or Enthought.
key_points: Cython can compile Python 3 code (including type annotations and f-strings) into C extensions that work with Python 2
Large institutions face much harder Python 2-to-3 transitions than individual developers due to production codebase complexity
The approach has significant limitations: no asyncio support, semantic differences, difficult introspection, and import compatibility issues
A commercial conda channel of Cython-compiled Python 2-compatible packages could be a viable business opportunity
While technically possible, extending Python 2 support may only delay the inevitable transition institutions must make
money_quotes: Many popular Python packages are dropping support for Python 2 next month. This will be painful for several large institutions.
For most developers in the ecosystem this isn't a problem. However, for larger companies or government organizations it's often far harder to switch.
Just because we can support Python 2 in this way doesn't mean that we should.
Long term, institutions do need to drop Python 2 and either move on to Python 3 or to some other language. Tricks like using Cython only extend the inevitable.
For me personally, seeing Cython as an option to provide continued support makes me much more comfortable with dropping Python 2.
I also think that maintaining a conda channel of Cython-compiled Python-2-compatible packages would be an excellent effort for a for-profit company.
key_insight: Cython offers a little-known escape hatch for institutions struggling with Python 2 migration, compiling Python 3 code into Python 2-compatible extensions, though this technical workaround shouldn't replace the fundamental need to modernize.
